title.plain_text: A time series interaction analysis method for building predictive models of learners using log data

tables.heading: Summary Table PRED
rows: Achieving a Passing Grade in MOOC Summative Evaluation

2 Literatures Courses
3 Internet Courses
3 Philosophy courses
3 Networks Courses
[21 features describing the accesses to different resources in different days of the week, 480 boolean features, 717 n-gram related features]

The work creates sequences of features. Authors attempt 4 distinct intervals:
daily, every 3 days, weekly and monthly.

For each period, boolean indicators are created mentioning whether a resource is accessed or not. Moreover, 2 to 5-grams are also created to mirror sequences of clicks in a specific activity.

Random undersampling of majority class (Not passing) for models to answer RQ1 and RQ2.

Authors create boolean flags for sequences of different length. Moreover, the first 2 questions focus on prediction at the end of the course, with the 3rd focusing on early prediction.

Work does not aim to find best model and best hyper-parameters. Instead it focuses on optimizing preprocessing strategy.

Authors point to the models created having minimal explanatory power even though they are decision trees. 

Model assessment is also rough:
1 - test performance on same course and likely same data
2. Holdout where test set is third offering of a course.

Models clearly overfit. Still, optimal time to make early predictions seems to be after 21 days, when the FPR tends to decrease substantially.

Models are course-specific but meant to generalize across different editions.

No feature selection was performed.

Does not use any algorithms beside DT.

Unverified assumption that previous editions work similarly to later editions of a same course.

blocks.text: Abstract

text.content: Abstract

rich_text.plain_text: Abstract

blocks.text: Need for new methods of predicting achievement that are cheaper and generalizable to increasingly large courses (e.g. MOOCs). Paper presents a novel approach of feature extraction from logs → Features extracted using proposed methodology are built from interactions between learners and resources.

blocks.text: Introduction

text.content: Introduction

rich_text.plain_text: Introduction

blocks.text: Current models for rpediction of student performance often depend on domain knowledge and pedagogical constraints which makes these models only effective in situations where the technologie or pedagogical strategies being measured are used. Thus, there is a need for more generalizability.

text.content: Current models for rpediction of student performance often depend on domain knowledge and pedagogical constraints which makes these models only effective in situations where the technologie or pedagogical strategies being measured are used. Thus, there is a need for more generalizability.

Thus, there is a need for more generalizability.

blocks.text: The authors intend to build a model from interactions that is generalizable and not course-specific. A factor motivating this approach is the increasing availability of LMS systems that collecte clickstream data. Altough there are many approaches for this, trhe authors specifically focus on time series interaction analysis with the goal of predicting summative evaluations on MOOCs.

blocks.text: Historically, prior performance data is more predictive of student performance than clickstream data. However, due to their availability, clickstream summaries have been more and more popular. For example, in MOOC, institutions often have very little knowledge on a student’s performance in previous courses. Thus behavioral data is the primary dsata source to form a predictive model until the end of examinations (at least in MOOC).

blocks.text: Approach

text.content: Approach

rich_text.plain_text: Approach

blocks.text: There are multiple perspectives when it comes to modelling cognitive skills or learning. The basis for many ITS is the ACT-R model that postulates that cognitive skilsl can be described as if they were mere production rules. Another theory argues that learning comes from making mistakes. Moreover, there is contact with materials or communication with peers.

blocks.text: N-Gram usage: The authors postulate that some co-occurrences of learning resources can be predictive of student success. To capture how some sequences of interactions may lead to performance, the authors explore the use n-gram → specifically 2-grams to 5-grams.

blocks.text: To test the feasibility, the authors opted to use a J48 DT. on 4 different MOOCs from Michigan University on Coursera. The models will be evaluated across the numbers of correct and incorrect predictions and Fleiss’s kappa. This metric has some constraints related with its interpretation that will be addressed later by the authors.
